title: What is complex systems science?
gist: David Krakauer's framing essay defines complex systems science as the study of adaptive phenomena hidden in plain sight — cities, economies, ecosystems, nervous systems — whose difficulty comes not from being too small or distant but from nonlinearity, randomness, collective dynamics, hierarchy, and emergence, which defeat both intuition and instruments. The bulk of the page is a curated foundational reading list of the field.

insights.claim: Complex systems differ from classical scientific objects in that they are directly perceivable rather than hidden by space or time; their difficulty arises instead from nonlinearity, randomness, collective dynamics, hierarchy, and emergence.
insights.speaker: David Krakauer
insights.so_what: Frames why standard reductionist tools and intuition fail on economies, markets, and ecosystems — the analytical edge is in modeling emergence, not in finer measurement.
insights.quote: But the way in which complex phenomena are hidden, beyond masking by space and time, is through nonlinearity, randomness, collective dynamics, hierarchy, and emergence
insights.locator: But the way in which complex phenomena are hidden

insights.claim: Adaptive systems such as cities, economies, civilizations, the nervous system, the Internet, and ecosystems are grouped together as a single class of phenomena amenable to a shared complexity-science toolkit.
insights.speaker: David Krakauer
insights.so_what: The unifying claim behind SFI's cross-domain method — the same scaling/network tools used on biology transfer to markets and urban systems.
insights.quote: Examples of these complex systems include cities, economies, civilizations, the nervous system, the Internet, and ecosystems.
insights.locator: These are the phenomena that we study as complex systems

insights.claim: Unlike distant stars or minerals that require amplified optics to study, collective and individual behavior can be investigated modestly through direct observation or experiment.
insights.speaker: David Krakauer
insights.so_what: Implies the bottleneck in social/economic science is conceptual, not observational — progress comes from new models rather than new instruments.
insights.quote: behavior — both individual and collective — seems to present itself in ways that can be investigated rather modestly through observation or experiment
insights.locator: Paradoxically, the complex world is one that we can

insights.claim: The Santa Fe Institute works in nondisciplinary teams to invent new concepts that make complex reality tractable to science, a program it has pursued for thirty-five years.
insights.speaker: David Krakauer
insights.so_what: Signals the institutional source of much cross-disciplinary complexity research that feeds into network science, scaling laws, and agent-based economics.
insights.quote: working in highly diverse, nondisciplinary teams to invent new concepts to render up complex reality to science
insights.locator: Over the course of thirty-five years

insights.claim: Phil Anderson's 1972 'More is Different' reoriented science away from reductionism by establishing broken symmetry, emergence, and hierarchical structure as foundational to complexity.
insights.speaker: David Krakauer
insights.so_what: The canonical citation for why aggregate behavior cannot be inferred from components — the intellectual basis for emergent-systems thinking applied to markets.
insights.quote: It set a new course for complex systems science as a departure from reductionism.
